era_context: Late Old Kingdom Egypt (Dynasty VI); Early Dynastic III Mesopotamia with competing city-states; Indus Valley Civilisation in its mature phase. Cuneiform and hieroglyphic writing are well-established in river valleys. Most of the world remains preliterate or in early stages of writing development.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe and the Steppe (sparse archaeological evidence)
East Asia beyond the Yellow River (limited documentation)
The Americas (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain)

events.title: Reign of Pepi I (Meryre) or Merenre of Egypt — Old Kingdom Dynasty VI
events.description: This year falls within the reign of a Dynasty VI pharaoh, likely Pepi I (Meryre) or his successor Merenre, depending on chronological system. Pepi I is credited with military campaigns in Nubia and the Sinai, and with maintaining centralised pharaonic authority. His reign is attested in king lists and official inscriptions from tombs. The period is marked by continued Old Kingdom stability, though some evidence suggests gradual weakening of central authority toward the end of the dynasty.
key_figures: Pepi I (Meryre) or Merenre — Pharaoh of Egypt
events.certainty_note: The identity and exact dating of the reigning pharaoh in -3091 depends on chronological assumptions. Conventional chronology places Pepi I c. 2278–2184 BCE; the year -3091 is approximate.
cross_references: Part of Old Kingdom Dynasty VI

events.title: Early Dynastic III city-state competition in Mesopotamia
events.description: The Early Dynastic III period in Mesopotamia is characterised by competition among city-states (Lagash, Umma, Uruk, Ur, Nippur, Kish) for regional dominance. Inscriptions from multiple cities attest to military campaigns, territorial disputes, and the consolidation of power by individual rulers. The period sees the development of more centralised city-state administrations and the emergence of kingship as a dominant political form. Trade networks connect Mesopotamia to the Indus Valley, the Levant, and Egypt.
key_figures: Various Ensi (governors) and kings of Mesopotamian city-states

sources.name: Contemporary inscriptions from city-states (Lagash, Umma, Uruk)
Archaeological evidence from excavations at Uruk, Lagash, Ur

events.certainty_note: The Early Dynastic III period is well-attested archaeologically and epigraphically. The year -3091 falls within this period, though specific events for this year are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: Chronological uncertainty is the primary issue. The conventional dating of Dynasty VI pharaohs varies by ±100–200 years depending on the chronological system used. Some scholars argue for a 'short chronology' that would place these events significantly later. The identity of the reigning pharaoh in -3091 is uncertain without specifying a chronological system.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -3091 is sparse and heavily dependent on king lists and inscriptions. Egyptian chronology for the Old Kingdom is notoriously uncertain. Mesopotamian chronology is similarly debated. The surviving sources are biased toward elite, literate cultures; most of the world is archaeologically invisible or poorly documented.

graph_edges.note: Reign of Dynasty VI pharaoh continues or transitions to successor
